findings.suggestion: Only redirect to same-origin relative paths. Reject or ignore any value that is not a path beginning with a single `/` (block `//` and backslash variants), e.g. `const safe = typeof next === 'string' && /^\/(?!\/)/.test(next) ? next : '/dashboard'; return res.redirect(safe);`. Alternatively validate against an explicit allowlist of permitted destinations.
findings.summary: Lines 9–11: `const next = req.query.next;` then `return res.redirect(next);` — the redirect target is fully attacker-controlled with no allowlist or same-origin check. Express's res.redirect honors absolute (`https://evil.example`) and protocol-relative (`//evil.example`) URLs, so `/login?next=https://evil.example` sends the victim off the trusted origin. The code confirms exactly the bug its own comment (lines 6–8) describes; the guard the comment says is missing is in fact absent.
findings.title: Open redirect: unvalidated `next` query param passed to res.redirect

findings.suggestion: Add a test (e.g. supertest) asserting off-origin values (`https://evil.example`, `//evil.example`) do not redirect externally and a valid relative `next` still redirects, then run it and attach the output before marking the security fix done.
findings.summary: The diff adds a new /login route (source.js:5-14) with no accompanying test and no verification artifact; the pre-review gate `rerun_relevant_verification` remains blocked (evidence lead `no-fresh-verification-evidence`). Nothing proves the redirect behavior — safe or unsafe — was exercised.
findings.title: No test or fresh verification for the new endpoint

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single new file implementing an Express /login handler with a genuine, exploitable open redirect on line 11. Assumption integrity: the code's comment (lines 6–8) accurately describes the vulnerability and the implementation confirms it — intent and code agree, so no misleading premise is planted for a follow-up agent. However the confirmed assumption is the defect itself, so it is reported as a high-severity security bug. No runtime verification was possible for this isolated file; finding is proven by static reading.

The 14-line change introduces a classic open-redirect: the `next` query param flows directly into res.redirect() with no validation (lines 9-11), allowing attacker-controlled absolute/protocol-relative URLs. From a product-safety lens this breaks the login return-flow and is a phishing vector — real user-facing harm. Self-documented as a BUG in the code comments; confirmed against the actual file. Verification gate (rerun_relevant_verification) remains open: I did not execute a request; the defect is static-definitive per Express semantics, and the smallest confirming artifact is a supertest assertion on the Location header.

Single new 14-line Express file intentionally demonstrating an open redirect. The vulnerability described in the comments is real and confirmed on lines 9-11: user-controlled `req.query.next` flows unvalidated into `res.redirect`. High-severity CWE-601. No tests or verification accompany the change (verification gate still blocked). Primary fix: restrict redirects to same-origin relative paths, rejecting `//` and `/\` prefixes, and add regression tests.
